title: Iran Formalizes Hormuz Toll System as Oil Prices Break $111
subhead: Tehran launches the Persian Gulf Strait Authority and a Bitcoin-backed insurance scheme to monetize the Hormuz closure, while global inventories near critical levels and Europe faces potential shortages within weeks.

sections.heading: Iran Institutionalizes the Hormuz Toll
sections.body:
Iran's Supreme National Security Council officially announced the formation of the Persian Gulf Strait Authority (PGSA) on Monday, formalizing Tehran's control over the vital waterway. The new body will provide "real-time updates on Hormuz Strait operations" and requires all ships to obtain transit authorization and pay tolls before entering the strait.

The PGSA has already begun sending "Vessel Information Declaration" forms to shipping operators, demanding detailed records of ownership, insurance, crew details, and intended transit routes. According to Lloyd's List, the authority will approve transits before ships enter the strait — transforming what was once an international waterway into a toll road under Iranian sovereignty.

Iranian parliamentarian Ebrahim Azizi said Tehran has "prepared a professional mechanism to manage traffic" through the strait, with the system designed to generate revenue while maintaining leverage over global energy flows.
sections.whyItMatters: Tehran is converting temporary wartime disruption into a permanent revenue and leverage mechanism. The PGSA institutionalizes what international law prohibits — unilateral control over an international strait — and creates a framework that persists regardless of ceasefire outcomes.

sections.heading: Bitcoin-Backed 'Hormuz Safe' Insurance Scheme Launches
sections.body:
Iran unveiled 'Hormuz Safe', a digital maritime insurance platform that offers ships safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for cryptocurrency payments. The scheme, developed by Iran's Ministry of Economy, aims to generate up to $10 billion in revenue according to Iranian authorities.

Ship operators choose a policy, pay premiums in Bitcoin or other cryptocurrencies, and receive digital proof of coverage via blockchain verification. The insurance covers collisions, cargo damage, accidents, and environmental incidents during transit.

However, experts warn the scheme faces serious obstacles. Abdul Khalique of Liverpool John Moores University Maritime Centre told Al Jazeera that Iran "will face serious financial, legal, and operational obstacles" due to sanctions restricting access to global reinsurance markets. "Without credible reinsurance, shipowners may doubt whether claims would actually be paid," Khalique said. International maritime regulators may also reject Iranian-issued certificates.

The scheme exists in a legal gray zone — Iran's toll requirements violate the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), though neither the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea nor the International Court of Justice can enforce rulings against Tehran.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is building a parallel financial architecture around its chokepoint control, using cryptocurrency to bypass sanctions while monetizing the disruption. The scheme tests whether blockchain-based insurance can function without traditional reinsurance backing.

sections.heading: Oil Breaks $111 as Markets Brace for Shortages
sections.body:
Brent crude climbed to $111.50 per barrel on Monday, up 2%, while WTI reached $108.20. The surge came after drone attacks on UAE and Saudi infrastructure and the failure of Trump's China visit to produce an Iran breakthrough.

Physical markets are tightening rapidly. The International Energy Agency's latest Oil Market Report estimates a 6 million barrel per day gap between supply and demand from March to June. JPMorgan warns OECD inventories could approach "operational stress levels" by early June.

Jeff Currie, executive co-chairman at Abaxx Commodity Exchange, told CNBC that physical shortages could hit Europe "any day now." He warned that once shortages materialize, prices will go "non-linear" as buyers compete for remaining supply. "Then we find out what the willingness is of somebody to pay for that last molecule," Currie said.
sections.whyItMatters: The market is pricing in prolonged disruption rather than temporary shortage. With inventories falling at a 1970s-depletion pace and no diplomatic breakthrough in sight, the physical crunch is arriving before price signals have fully reflected scarcity.

sections.heading: Europe Faces 52-Day Lag Even If Hormuz Reopens
sections.body:
Societe Generale analysts warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens by early June, the physical supply chain would require at least 52 days to normalize. The sequence — tanker transit, discharge, refining, and distribution — means millions of barrels per day remain offline regardless of diplomatic progress.

A late June reopening would push physical relief to late August, with meaningful normalization not expected until September. Extended disruption could push oil prices toward $150 per barrel, keeping prices elevated through 2026 and into 2027.

European inventories are depleting at their fastest pace since the 1970s oil crisis. German Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil told reporters that the G7 is the "right forum to discuss ending the Iran conflict," calling the Hormuz blockade "a serious threat to the global economy."
sections.whyItMatters: The lag between diplomatic resolution and physical relief means the worst supply crunch may come after any ceasefire. Markets and governments are realizing that reopening the strait is necessary but insufficient to end the energy crisis.

sections.heading: Australian LNG Strike Threatens Second Front
sections.body:
Australian unions filed strike notice at Inpex's Ichthys LNG facility for a two-week shutdown starting May 27. The Offshore Alliance said 243 stoppages and work bans could disrupt operations through June 10, after negotiations over wages and working conditions collapsed.

The threat comes as global LNG markets are already strained by the Iran war. Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex, the world's largest, has been damaged by Iranian strikes, costing QatarEnergy an estimated $20 billion annually in lost revenue and requiring up to five years to repair.

Ichthys produces approximately 8.9 million tonnes of LNG annually, primarily supplying Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan. A two-week outage would remove roughly 350,000 tonnes from an already tight market.
sections.whyItMatters: A supply squeeze on two fronts — Hormuz and Australia — would compound LNG scarcity just as Northern Hemisphere summer demand peaks. Asian importers reliant on both Gulf and Australian supply face a double squeeze.

sections.heading: US Sanctions Waiver for Russian Oil Expires
sections.body:
The US Treasury Department allowed a sanctions waiver permitting purchases of Russian oil stranded at sea to expire on May 16 without renewal. The temporary license had allowed countries to continue purchasing Russian cargoes despite broader sanctions imposed after Moscow's invasion of Ukraine.

The waiver was initially introduced to prevent market disruption during the Iran war's early weeks. Its expiration closes a narrow window for Russian oil purchases while global supply remains constrained.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had previously said there were no plans to renew the authorization. The move effectively tightens sanctions enforcement on Moscow at a moment when elevated global oil prices are already boosting Russian energy revenues.
sections.whyItMatters: The waiver's expiration removes a pressure valve from the global oil market. Russian supply that was marginally accessible becomes fully sanctioned, tightening an already strained market while forcing Washington to choose between enforcing Ukraine-related sanctions and managing Hormuz-induced scarcity.

sections.heading: Moody's Warns of Permanent Energy Market Restructuring
sections.body:
Moody's Ratings issued a report warning that Hormuz disruption is becoming a "new normal" rather than a temporary shock. The agency now assumes "prolonged and significant disruption through autumn" as its central scenario.

Shipping through the strait has fallen by more than 90% from pre-conflict levels. Moody's expects Brent to remain between $90-110 per barrel for much of this year, with sustained prices reducing GDP growth by 0.2-0.8 percentage points for major economies.

"Global shipping routes are being structurally rewired," Moody's said. Countries are turning to non-Gulf suppliers, alternative pipeline routes, and regional trade systems to reduce Hormuz dependency. India, which sources 46% of its crude from the Middle East, faces the highest exposure among major economies.

The agency warned that "some structural shifts in supply chain design, risk premiums and defense spending will probably be permanent" — regardless of when the conflict ends.
sections.whyItMatters: Major financial institutions are pricing in permanent change rather than recovery. The assumption that Hormuz disruption is temporary is giving way to planning for a post-Hormuz energy architecture.

bottomLine: Iran moved to institutionalize its chokepoint control through the PGSA and a cryptocurrency-based insurance scheme, converting wartime leverage into permanent revenue infrastructure. Oil prices broke $111 as IEA data confirmed a 6 million barrel daily supply-demand gap, with strategists warning that Europe faces physical shortages within weeks. Even if Hormuz reopens tomorrow, a 52-day supply chain lag means the worst may be yet to come. The crisis is shifting from acute disruption to structural realignment.
sources: Oilprice.com, CNBC, Channel News Asia, Firstpost, Gulf News, Kyiv Independent, Guardian, Reuters, IEA, Societe Generale, Moody's Ratings, JPMorgan, Abaxx Commodity Exchange, Lloyd's List, Al Jazeera
